title: Setting Fees As A Healer Without Guilt
dek: *The knot in your chest when someone asks your rate isn't about greed. It's unfelt rage at a culture that taught you to give until empty, dressed up as humility. Here's how to untangle price from worthiness, honor your own nervous system, and charge what keeps the lights on without performing scarcity or abundance.*
bodyMarkdown:
TL;DR. Healer guilt around money is stored anger turned inward, preventing you from sustaining the work people need. Clear pricing reflects the metabolic cost of holding space, not the commodification of spirit. When you name your worth without apology, you model the self-regard your clients are learning to practice.

She's sitting across from a potential client in a coffee shop, hands wrapped around a cooling mug, and the question comes: "So what do you charge?" Her throat tightens. She hears herself say a number twenty dollars lower than she planned, watches the relief wash over the other person's face, and feels a hot shame bloom in her sternum. Later, alone in her car, she'll wonder why she did that again. What she won't name yet is the anger, the old stored fury at being told her gifts should come free because they come naturally, the inherited belief that asking for money makes the healing less holy. ## The Body Keeps the Ledger In our experience working with healers, bodyworkers, and practitioners across modalities, the physical sensation of naming a fee is rarely neutral. Your shoulders might rise toward your ears. Your jaw might clench. You might feel a sudden urge to justify, to explain, to offer a discount before anyone asks. Peter Levine's work on trauma and the nervous system shows us that these aren't just thoughts, they're procedural memories, body-held scripts about safety and threat. When you were small and asked for what you needed, what happened? Did the room go cold? Did someone sigh? Did you learn that wanting made you a burden? The guilt you feel around money isn't a moral compass. It's a somatic echo. Somewhere along the way, your nervous system learned that receiving compensation for care was dangerous. Maybe you watched a parent struggle. Maybe you absorbed the idea that spiritual work should be donation-based, that sliding scales are the only ethical model, that if you charge full price you're excluding the people who need you most. These beliefs live in your tissue, not just your mind. They show up as a clenched stomach when you send an invoice, a tight chest when you see your bank balance, a vague nausea when you think about raising your rates. Pat Ogden's sensorimotor psychotherapy teaches us that the body holds procedural knowledge, the "how" of navigating the world. If your body learned that asking for money equals rejection or judgment, then every pricing conversation becomes a moment of activation. You're not just setting a boundary. You're confronting an old survival pattern that says staying small keeps you safe. ## What Anger Has to Do with Any of This Here's what no one tells you: guilt is often anger's polite mask. When you feel guilty about charging, you're actually furious. Furious that you have to justify your worth. Furious that clients comparison-shop you against Reiki practitioners on Groupon. Furious that you spent years training, that you hold people's grief and trauma and terror in your body for hours afterward, and someone still balks at a hundred-dollar session. Robert Augustus Masters writes about how we "nice" our anger into submission, turning it into self-blame because that feels safer than directing it outward. When you drop your rate in the moment, you're not being generous. You're swallowing rage. [The APA's research summary on anger management](https://www.apa.org/topics/anger/control) notes that suppressed anger often manifests as guilt, resentment, and passive behaviors. The healer who chronically undercharges isn't avoiding greed. She's avoiding the full-throated roar of "I deserve to be paid for this work." That roar feels dangerous. It feels unfeminine, unspiritual, unloving. So she turns the anger inward, makes it about her own inadequacy, and charges seventy-five dollars when she needs one-fifty to pay rent. Across the articles we've published on this site, we return again and again to the idea that unfelt anger becomes the water you swim in. It shapes your choices without you noticing. You overbook yourself. You see clients back-to-back without breaks. You answer texts at midnight. You tell yourself this is service, but your body is screaming. When you finally let yourself feel the anger underneath the guilt, something shifts. You stop negotiating your worth in real time. You stop performing scarcity as proof of your humility. ## The Metabolic Cost of Holding Space Let's get practical. Healing work, whatever your modality, has a metabolic cost. Your nervous system co-regulates with your client's. If you're working with trauma, your body is tracking micro-signals of threat and safety for sixty or ninety minutes straight. That's not passive. That's labor. After a session, you might feel wrung out, need to lie down, crave salt or protein or silence. This isn't weakness. It's biology. When you set your fee, you're not just covering your time in the room. You're covering the recovery time after, the training that taught you to do this safely, the supervision or therapy that keeps you clear, the [Doctor's Best L-Theanine with Suntheanine 150 mg](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B001OXTGVG?tag=spankyspinola-20) you take to settle your system after a hard day, the bath salts and the quiet and the two hours you need to be human again before you make dinner. You're covering the invisible work of not carrying your clients' stories into your own relationships, of maintaining boundaries that protect both of you, of knowing when to refer out. In my own practice, I started tracking my actual costs per session: not just rent and supplies, but the energetic and somatic toll. I noticed I needed at least thirty minutes between clients to ground, to shake out my hands, to drink water and look out the window. I needed one full day a week with no sessions at all. I needed monthly bodywork and quarterly supervision. When I added it up, my "hourly rate" wasn't hourly at all. It was covering a whole network of care that made the work sustainable. Your pricing isn't arbitrary. It's a direct reflection of what it costs you, in real terms, to show up clear and present. When you charge less than that cost, you're borrowing from your future self. You're saying, "I'll just push through, I'll be fine, I don't need as much as I need." That's not generosity. That's self-abandonment. ## The Practice: Name the Number Out Loud Here's something you can do in the next five minutes. Stand up. Feel your feet on the floor. Take a breath into your low belly. Now say out loud, to the empty room, "My rate is [the number you actually need]." Don't explain it. Don't justify it. Just say the number and notice what happens in your body. Does your throat tighten? Does your face flush? Do you want to laugh it off, add "but I offer sliding scale" before anyone asks? Just notice. Now say it again. "My rate is [number]." Say it five times, ten times, until it starts to feel like information instead of confession. Let your body learn that naming your worth doesn't make the sky fall. This is somatic practice, the same way we teach in [the fire toolkit](/fire-toolkit). You're updating an old program, one repetition at a time. ## When You Raise Your Rates, You Raise Your Standards There's a strange alchemy that happens when you charge what you're worth. The clients who show up are different. They're more committed. They do the homework. They show up on time. They take the work seriously because they've invested seriously. You're not trying to convince anyone of your value. The price does that translation for you. This doesn't mean you never work on sliding scale or trade. It means your baseline, your standard rate, reflects the true cost of your work. From that solid ground, you can make conscious choices about when and how to offer accessibility. But when your baseline is already too low, every session becomes a subtle betrayal of your own needs. You resent the people you're trying to help. You burn out and blame them for not valuing you, when the truth is you didn't value yourself first. Over the years I've seen practitioners double their rates and watch their practices not shrink but stabilize. The clients who leave weren't the right fit anyway. The ones who stay or arrive are meeting you as a peer, not as a savior or a charity. There's a respect in the exchange that wasn't there before. You stop feeling like you have to prove yourself every session. You've already been chosen, at full price, and that changes the energy in the room. Some of your best support might come from the plant allies in [our herbal cabinet](/herbs), the ones that help you stay grounded in your own authority. Or the [THORNE - 5-HTP - Contains Vitamin B6 - Serotonin Support - 90 Capsules](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B00GDXHBS2?tag=spankyspinola-20) that some practitioners find helpful for mood regulation when old shame patterns surface. These aren't fixes. They're support structures while you do the deeper work of reclaiming your worth. ## The Story You Tell About Money You have a story about money. Everyone does. Maybe it's "there's never enough." Maybe it's "people like me don't get to have wealth." Maybe it's "spiritual people don't care about money" or "if I charge too much I'm just like the system I'm trying to heal." These stories run in the background, shaping every decision you make about pricing, about how many clients you see, about whether you buy the good coffee or the cheap one. Robert Augustus Masters reminds us that we often confuse boundaries with cruelty, softness with boundarylessness. When you hold a clear price, you're not being hard. You're being honest. You're saying, "This is what it costs me to show up for you fully." The story that says clear boundaries are unloving is a story designed to keep you compliant, to keep you giving until you're empty. Start noticing the story you tell. Write it down. Then ask: who taught me this? Was it true then? Is it true now? Can I write a new story that includes both my care for others and my care for myself? A story where receiving money doesn't cancel out the healing, where financial sustainability and spiritual integrity aren't opposites? ## The Clients Who Push Back Someone will push back. Someone will say your rate is too high, will tell you about their own financial struggles, will hint that a real healer wouldn't charge so much. This is the moment your inner work gets tested. Can you stay anchored in your worth without defending it? Can you offer a referral to someone in their price range without dropping your rate to prove you're still a good person? The pushback is information. It tells you who's ready to work with you and who isn't. It's not personal, even when it feels personal. Some people genuinely can't afford you, and that's real, and you can hold compassion for that without making it your responsibility to fix. Other people can afford you but are used to negotiating, testing, seeing if you'll bend. When you don't, you teach them something about your boundaries that will serve the work if they decide to proceed. In moments of pushback, [Bach Rescue Remedy (alt SKU)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B000WVWB40?tag=spankyspinola-20) can be a quick somatic reset, a way to come back to your center when you feel the old pattern of appeasing start to kick in. But the real remedy is practice. Each time you hold your rate without apologizing, you're rewiring the belief that your worth is negotiable. ## Building a Practice That Lasts Sustainability isn't a luxury. It's the foundation. If you burn out in two years because you undercharged and overworked, you're not serving anyone. The people who need you next year, in five years, won't have access to you because you didn't build a practice that could hold your actual life. That includes your need for rest, for your own therapy, for vacations, for a retirement account, for the occasional meal out without checking your bank balance first. When you price sustainably, you're not just thinking about today's session. You're thinking about the version of yourself ten years from now who's still doing this work because she learned early how to resource herself. You're building something that can weather a slow season, a health crisis, a global pandemic. You're not scrambling for clients or saying yes to people who aren't a fit because you're desperate for the income. This long view requires you to trust that there are enough people who value your work at full price. That trust doesn't come from positive thinking. It comes from practice, from the evidence you gather each time someone pays your rate without flinching, each time your bank account stabilizes, each time you realize you made it through the month without panic. The trust builds slowly, session by session, invoice by invoice. If you're interested in more writing on building sustainable practices that honor both your gifts and your humanity, [theoraclelover.com](https://theoraclelover.com) explores these themes through a slightly different lens. ## FAQ: What Practitioners Actually Ask ### How do I explain my rates without sounding defensive? You don't explain. You state. "My rate is $150 for a ninety-minute session." If they ask why, you can say, "That rate reflects my training, experience, and the time I hold for integration after our work together." Keep it factual, not apologetic. Your tone teaches them whether your price is negotiable. If you sound uncertain, they'll hear an invitation to haggle. ### What if I live in an area where no one charges what I need to charge? Then you might need to work remotely with some clients, or specialize in a way that justifies a premium, or accept that you'll have fewer clients at a higher rate rather than more clients at a rate that doesn't cover your costs. Geography matters, but so does positioning. If you're the only person in your area doing a specific kind of trauma-informed bodywork, you're not competing with the general market. You're creating your own. ### Should I offer sliding scale, and if so, how? Sliding scale works when you have a baseline rate that's already sustainable and you designate a specific percentage of your practice for reduced-fee spots. Don't make your entire practice sliding scale unless you have other income. Be clear about what qualifies: is it based on income? On acute need? Have a policy and stick to it. And remember, sliding scale is a choice you make from abundance, not a default you offer from guilt. ## The Room You're Building Picture this: a year from now, you're sitting in your office between clients. There's twenty minutes before the next person arrives. You're sipping tea, looking out the window, feeling the ground under your feet. Your bank account is stable. You're not panicking about rent. You're not overbooked to the point of exhaustion. You said no to two potential clients this month because they weren't the right fit, and you didn't feel guilty. You raised your rates six months ago, and three people left, and twelve people stayed, and five new ones arrived who value exactly what you offer. This isn't fantasy. It's what happens when you stop negotiating your worth in real time, when you let your pricing reflect the true cost of your work, when you trust that the people who are meant to work with you will find a way. The anger you've been swallowing transforms into clarity. The guilt becomes a signal, a reminder to check in: am I honoring my own needs here, or am I performing generosity at my own expense? You're not just setting fees. You're building a life where your gifts can flourish because you've stopped starving the person who carries them. That's not selfish. That's the whole point. The world needs you resourced, rested, and here for the long haul. Your pricing is how you make that possible. ## Keep reading - [How to Have a Real Fight Without Damage](/article/how-to-have-a-real-fight-without-damage) - [Forgiveness Without Pretending: A Realistic Map](/article/forgiveness-without-pretending-a-realistic-map) - [How to Negotiate Domestic Labor Without Combat](/article/how-to-negotiate-domestic-labor-without-combat) ---
Written by **The Oracle Lover**, independent essayist on anger, somatics, and embodied spirituality. Last updated May 29, 2026. In our work on setting fees as a healer without guilt, we keep returning to one quiet truth: the body has been telling you something for a long time, and it deserves a thoughtful listener. The Anger Practice is a companion journal to [theoraclelover.com](https://theoraclelover.com), and every piece here is written with you in mind.
